title: OCR Online
tagline: Browser-side OCR for PDFs and images.
summary: PDFs and images are processed locally. The pipeline lifts text from the PDF's own text layer where one exists, and runs PP-OCRv5 mobile on raster pages otherwise. There is no server.

sections.heading: Pipeline
items: PDF pages with an embedded text layer are read with pdf.js. No model is invoked.
Scanned pages and images are processed by PP-OCRv5 mobile (detection + recognition) running in a Web Worker.
Results stream into the queue as pages finish; the queue runs at most two documents in parallel to bound memory.

sections.heading: Model + runtime
items: PP-OCRv5 mobile, served from media.githubusercontent.com (≈17 MB).
onnxruntime-web wasm, served from cdn.jsdelivr.net (≈26 MB).
Both are HTTP-cached by the browser after first load; a service worker keeps them available offline.

sections.heading: Network surface
items: Three external hosts are contacted at startup: cdn.jsdelivr.net, media.githubusercontent.com, raw.githubusercontent.com.
After load, no network activity is required for recognition.
The header counter splits cross-origin requests into allowlisted (model + runtime) and unknown.

sections.heading: Confidentiality
items: Documents are held in memory in the open tab. Closing the tab releases them.
There is no backend processing component.
Both claims are checkable with the browser's DevTools network and memory panels.

faq.a: Yes. There is no backend, so there is no per-request cost on the host side. Static assets and model weights are served by public CDNs.
PaddleOCR PP-OCRv5 mobile, via ppu-paddle-ocr and onnxruntime-web.
Pages are rasterised one at a time and processed in parallel by the worker pool. Files above 500 MB require explicit confirmation to avoid out-of-memory failures.
Yes. A hidden iframe + postMessage SDK is published at /embed.html and /embed-client.js. See /embed-demo.html.
No. The pipeline is entirely client-side. Verify in the browser's network tab during processing.
